sentences.text: Hello.
My name is Freda Rosenblatt.
In 19-- February 1, 1932 in Bessarabia, at that time was part of Romania-- province of Romania.
Yes.
That's right-- on the map, I did-- Bessarabia.
Now it's Moldova.
As a child, my father was very well off.
They have a factory from sunflower oil, sunflower seeds.
And my mother was a very educated lady.
She came by herself from Ukraine during the revolution.
And I was grow up with maids and nannies.
And that's what I remember until 1940.
Chuna.
Sara.
Yes, a older brother.
His name was Hersch, but was nicknamed him [INAUDIBLE] as a child.
Yes, about four, five years, approximately.
Yes, as a child-- I mean, going to school, playing with children, normal-- normal life.
Romanian school-- at that time was Romania.
In school was Romanian.
But we spoke home Yiddish and Russian.
Because Bessarabia once belonged to Russia until the First World War.
So the majority of the-- of the people spoke Yiddish and Russian, and some Romanian.
So we-- because my mother was from Russia, we spoke Russian.
We also have a maid home who spoke Russian and Yiddish.
So this was my main-- main languages what we spoke home.
Do you remember the factory that your father ran?
Oh yeah.
As a child, I used to go to visit.
The-- the maid used to take me there.
It was a beautiful home and had a beautiful life.
Beautiful, big garden with fruit, with trees-- it's like a little paradise right behind our home.
Before the war was about 5,000, 6,000 Jews.
I didn't know the rest, but Jews have approximately 5,000, 6,000 Jews.
No, I wouldn't say.
It was a modern Zionist town.
Yes.
Oh yes, yes, yes.
Actually, the war in our place started in '41.
Actually, the Russian occupied Bessarabia in 1940.
They gave an ultimatum to the Romanian in the 24 hours they won back Bessarabia.
And that's what occupied were the Russians.
It wasn't the war.
They just took it over.
And everything was taken away from us, because my father was a wealthy man.
Our home was the KGB.
Our factory, the machinery was taken away to Russia.
Our furniture went to the mayor of the city.
Our grandfather clock went into the income tax department.
So everything was taken away from us.
And my parents-- all of us supposed to be sent away to Siberia.
So my parents moved to a bigger city named Cernauti.
Because there's a big city, so maybe will-- we'll be able to escape to send away to-- to Siberia.
So this was in 1941.
In Cernauti, we stayed not even a year.
Because in 1941, the Germany with Romania-- at that time Romania became already part of Germany.
And they attacked the Soviet Union.
And we-- we were on the border with Romania, so right away Cernauti, Bessarabia was occupied by the Romanian, the Germans.
So the Jews from Bessarabia itself were already way-- first of all, the first three days, were told to the Romanian, do anything you want to the Jews.
You can kill.
You can murder.
You can burn.
You can rape-- anything, because Jews are not people, period.
We were in Cernauti.
In Cernauti, they didn't send us right away.
We weren't deported right away.
We were deported in October.
So from Cernauti, in approximately October '41, the deportation to Transnistria started.
My father wasn't allowed to work.
Because he was branded as a capitalist, a kulak, enemy of the Russian-- the Soviets.
So what we still had-- [? diamond ?] [? wristband, ?] some money-- so that's why we're living from beginning of-- middle of '40 till '41 when the war started.
So then when the war started in the Romania when the Germans came, all Cernauti were put in a ghetto.
We exactly were living in a area where belonged to the ghetto.
So the people from out of the ghetto came to live with us, a few families.
Who know how many people were living together.
And then in October, they started the deportation.
Nobody knew where we are going, where we are taken.
The only thing-- we were taken to the train station, put in the cattle train-- who knows how many people-- and we were taken to Marculesti.
I don't remember how many days we were travel by train.
And we were taken to Marculesti.
This is Bessarabia.
This the Romanian gendarme.
This is like the-- the SS, the German SS, something in this field under another name.
So we arrive to--
--to Marculesti.
Oh, god, terrible, terrible-- they were waiting for us at the train station.
It was October already in a evening, a rainy, cold day.
They were waiting for us with whips and butts.
And the way you-- you see the-- the Germans yelling, [SPEAKING GERAMN], the Romanian did the same things but in the Romanian language.
And they kicked us out from the train.
And we were taken to the village.
And the villagers were looking at us like we would be kind of a circus.
And everybody wore a knapsack with personal belongings were tearing off.
And nobody would-- everybody was afraid.
We were taken to the ghetto Marculesti-- and the barbed wire.
We stayed there for two weeks.
The mice, the rats, the misery-- it's unbelievable to imagine.
People were dying.
And everything was taken away from us.
The documentation-- they said, you are Jews.
Jews are not people.
You don't deserve the documentation.
Personal belongings, jewelry-- my brother had a beautiful violin he played before the war.
It was taken away from him.
This was Marculesti.
After two weeks, we were-- for-- this time we went on a forced march-- no train, no wagon, nothing, just forced march to go into a forest named [INAUDIBLE].
This already close to the River Dniester.
This from Transnistria with camps from the-- near Transnistria, the other side of the river Dniester.
Bug-- two rivers, Dniester and Bug.
On the other side, yes.
That's right.
This Ukraine, but they renamed it Transnistria during the--
That's right.
They-- they renamed it Transnistria.
Because it's part of Ukraine.
It was-- it was never existed something a name.
They gave it a name-- Transnistria.
But this was Ukraine.
This belonged to the Soviet Union before the war.
No.
Nothing.
They all used to say, Jews are Communists.
We are taking you to Stalin.
That's we were told-- you are going to Stalin, because you're Communist.
And the Romanians used to say, we are not going to kill you, but we are making to make your life so miserable that you are going to be begging for your death.
So they took us to the forest.
Marculesti was already November, approximately-- cold.
November-- it's snow.
And there were already some-- on the trees were people engraved their name.
People were before us engraved their names, so maybe their-- their memories.
There were mass graves.
Those were people were before died there and were buried in the mass graves in the [INAUDIBLE] Forest.
So we stayed there for a couple of days, and then were taken across the River Dniester.
About two days, it was winter.
It was cold.
And everybody was-- nobody knew was going to happen.
Where we got-- because we saw the mass graves.
We're going to be killed or-- nobody know.
I mean, they're panicky.
They're scared.
But really nobody knew what-- what's going to happen to us.
Hundreds and hundreds of people-- hundred.
And to cross the River Dniester, it was-- the bridge was practically shaking.
And people who couldn't walk, they were pushing into the River Dniester.
It was terrible.
It's impossible to describe.
Old, the young, children, sick people, and would beating us.
It's wasn't just slowly, go slowly.
They were with the rifles and with the butts, and screaming and yelling.
And it was panicky till we crossed the other side of the Dniester, this was Transnistria.
It was before the war Ukraine.
My parents were holding us-- me and my brother-- holding us tight.
Becomes somehow I don't know, people knew that in some cases they are separate-- let's say they call it the convoy-- have suddenly would separate, half a convoy right, half a convoy left.
And some people were behind, and they separated families.
I don't know from where people knew about it, but somehow.
Or-- or--
So my parents were holding me and my brother tight, protecting us from-- from the whips, and from the beatings, and from-- just protecting, because I was nine years old.
My brother was about, what, 14.
So that's what's happened till we crossed the River Dniester to the other side of the Transnistria.
And who welcome you on the other side?
The Romanian-- the gendarmes were all the time with us, because this was-- Transnistria became part of Romania.
They were divided because the Romanian were collaborating with the Germans.
So this part of the Ukraine, they give-- they gave it to the Romania.
And the Romania use it for one single purpose-- to bring Jews to their death from Bessarabia, Bukovina-- another province, and part of cities of Romania too.
So those-- this was-- And the Jews were living in Ukraine for generations.
So all those Jews were put into ghetto, death camps, labor camps and the people were brought in-- they were just to die.
But the most of us is because we were forced to march from place to place.
It was winter, no food.
The clothing those taken away by the villagers or whatever.
And-- and that was-- that was life.
People were dying on the street.
Nobody would bother even to look at the dead person, because everybody was afraid.
If somebody would try a little bit of water to pick up so there was enough, the gendarme hit him over the head with a rifle butt, and that was the end of it.
If somebody had some piece of clothes-- and the-- some of the-- this was already in the Ukraine-- they knew, let's say they could-- a good piece of clothes for a slice of bread.
We weren't thinking for the tomorrow.
Today, we're hungry.
Today, we're starving.
So they were coming, if the Romanian gendarme was just a little bit farther, we came-- here, you have a piece of bread.
Give me some of piece of clothes or something.
This was the food.
But that time was already snow on the ground.
We were eating snow, because there was no water.
So when we came from across the-- the Dniester, there was different places.
Because the before the war, this part of Ukraine, they was Soviet Union with the kolkhoz, the collective farms.
So those collective farms-- they were keeping the cows, the-- the horses, the pigs-- those were our homes-- became ours homes to sleep overnight.
And then the next day, farther.
This was from every day, we're in another place.
Do you remember sleeping in a barn?
Oh, definitely.
Sure, sure.
The is-- the first was [?
Yampol. ?] They were just crossing the-- the-- the border for the Transnistria-- to Transnistria, the Dniester, and then were another place, [INAUDIBLE], another barn.
And then when arrived-- arrived to the barn in Obodovka.
Then we met the people from Bessarabia.
Because we were taken from Cernauti.
This was part of Bukovina.
And we didn't know at all what happened with the people from Bessarabia.
Obodovka was a village.
And there was a big barn-- they call it the [NON-ENGLISH] where the people were bringing the cows to milk before the war.
And in the time of the Romanian, they made it a place for the Jews to live.
When I came close to this barn, I remember a woman came running out of that barn.
She said, you should have insisted to be killed.
Go inside and have a look what's there.
In one day, she lost her husband and her little boy.
We didn't know what she was talking about when they opened those big gates, because they were like for cows.
And they herd us in.
It was dark.
I didn't see anything.
Suddenly, I saw like a mountain.
I said, what is the mountain there?
When my-- when my eyes got used to the darkness, it was a mountain of corpses, dead Jewish people.
Young, old, children, naked, undressed were lying one on top of the other one.
At last, we were sitting down in the mud and in the dirt.
I saw a young girl was holding something covered.
I didn't know what it is.
She said, this is her dead mother.
She didn't want to throw it up there with the dead people, so she was sleeping, living to-- near her dead mother.
Did--
They weren't allowed.
They would just insist they have to lie there in the-- nobody-- The Romanian did not allow to take people to-- to bury those people.
Never mind a decent burial-- bury in a mass grave, but don't let them rot in such a condition.
So and the-- and the people around on the floor were mostly half dead already, because in such a condition, it was winter, cold.
And the next day, the people were still half a life were forced to march to another place.
This was near Bershad.
And Bershad was a ghetto.
But we passed by Bershad to another barn.
There-- there they were a place where pigs were kept before the war.
If was cold in winter.
And my parents realized that here in this barn, we would froze-- freeze to death.
Because from there, this was supposed to be there not to go nowhere.
We knew that this is to die.
So my parents thought we would go to Bershad.
This was a ghetto.
But it was not allowed to get out.
So they risked their lives.
We will die in this barn.
We will die no matter what.
So we walked about three or five kilometers.
I don't remember exactly.
We arrived to Bershad.
And Bershad at that time was a big epidemic of typhus.
So the place where my parents find to live-- it was like shack, a barn-- cold, miserable, lie down on the straw.
And in no time, my parent-- my father died exactly on my 10th birthday, February 1, 1942.
Do you remember him being sick?
He had the gangrene on both legs.
And I remember even the night when he died.
We were lying on the floor.
And a few days before this, my mother find out he had-- he had-- he had gangrene.
Because my mother was a medical student before the war.
And a doctor told her that my father had gangrene.
She knew as a medical student what gangrene is.
And I remember, she was crying and screaming.
And I didn't know why she was crying.
And I asked her, Ma, what's happening?
And she tells me in Russian, Father has gangrene.
And I sad, what is gangrene?
How should I know?
I was nine years old.
So on the night when he died, me and my brother were sleeping in the middle, my both parents on the sides so to keep us warm.
And my mother somehow had the feeling that my father was dead.
And she told my brother, have a look.
Is Father breathing?
And we started to push and said, dead.
Father doesn't breathe.
He was gone.
Three weeks-- three weeks later, my mother got-- my mother got sick of dysentery, three weeks later to the day.
She died too.
Were you able to bury them?
They were taken-- at that time in Bershad, there so many people died from all disease they didn't have any time to bury so many people.
So they take with a wagon, with a horse and buggy, throwing all the dead people on that wagon, out somewhere in a cemetery, throwing them to the mass grave.
I never seen-- I not-- I just remember they were thrown in this wagon.
But where they were buried, I never knew.
Because it was somewhere in a field.
The Jews weren't even allowed to go there.
I was-- I was very sick by then.
My brother was half and half, but I was at that time very sick.
My body was full with sores.
I was swollen.
I was crying.
I was dizzy.
I [INAUDIBLE] didn't even know-- I think I didn't know what happened to me.
I was just standing-- I think we're already so destroyed morally, even children, that that-- that person didn't mean anything.
Because wherever we were going to looking, people are dead.
People were all around.
People were dying on the street.
And at that winter was one of the very coldest winter remembered in the history-- so much snow, if a person will die-- you could-- they fall-- just exhaustion, he fell, the snow will cover him.
And he will freeze to death.
Nobody will bother to pick him up to help, because each time another person died.
So me and my brother were left alone.
I was 10.
My brother was about 15 years old.
So some people from my hometown-- they said that they are relatives, but their son left some clothes for my parents.
And at that time-- it might sound funny now-- but a piece of clothing means a piece of bread-- a slice of bread.
So he took us to-- to be with him.
And he told that me and my brother won't take long, and we will die too.
No, he didn't suppose to.
I mean, the way he treated us, it was understandable what-- what he meant.
Because he took right away over our parents, every our belongings.
Because whatever my parents had, they didn't had a chance even to exchange it because they die right away.
Maybe they would survive that first winter, because this first winter, '42-- people-- the most of the people will die was in this winter, because it was very cold.
And people were dropping dead right and left.
It was terrible.
So my parents even didn't had even a chance to exchange anything from their belonging-- maybe something, but that's about all.
And he took over all our parents' belongings and thought that it won't take long, we will be dead too.
But miracles, we both survived.
You mean from [INAUDIBLE].
Oh yeah, there were.
But people-- everybody was thinking for themselves.
People-- when you keep people like animals, eventually that will they become.
People were thinking for themselves.
People buried their own children.
Children buried their parents.
It was not a normal life.
People didn't think about others.
People think how to survive.
What the meaning to survival doesn't making any difference, just to survive.
So this was the first winter.
After I went through this, I was sick.
My-- I had frostbites.
I was filled with sores.
I was in such bad shape that it's a miracle how I survived.
After the first winter, my brother went to work in the factory where they make furniture just to work a-- The Ukrainian people got paid for it.
But the Jewish people who worked there worked for little pieces of wood or something.
I don't know.
I think they came from the factory to the ghetto-- because this was ghetto with barbed wire-- to give Jews to-- to-- to go-- they needed people to work for-- for free.
Because they had to pay for the Ukranian, for the non-Jews.
The Jewish people were working for no money.
So they took my brother to work there.
I went to-- in the-- in the village.
In order to go to the village, you had to cross a bridge, because it was a big river.
And also around that were barbed wire-- for some food.
And also I found a Ukrainian woman.
She needed a babysitter for her little boy.
To go there, we have to go three kilometer one way, three kilometer another way.
It was endangering my life.
Because the commandant-- the Romanian commandant generale-- he caught a 14-years-old boy in that village.
He went to beg some food.
So he tied him down to this motorcycle and dragged him all over the ghetto-- a 14-years-old boy to show an example what happen to a Jew when he goes out of the ghetto.
I was going every single day back and forth.
Walking, definitely.
If I'd be caught, I would have the same-- the same what happened to me like to this boy, or were even thrown alive into the river.
No, it was a open road, because there were a river.
There wasn't field.
There were a river in the middle-- but crossing the bridge.
And I endangered my life, because that was the meaning-- how will I survive?
And some of my food what I got there from I used to bring to my brother into the factory.
That's-- that's all, for food-- just for the food.
At that time-- it sounds funny now-- but if she gave me a little piece of bread, I remember she used to cook borscht all day long.
And this-- borscht, a luxury-- a piece of bread with borscht-- this is-- this-- this was I working for.
And I endangered my life many times.
She used to give me home some food.
I used to take to my brother into the factory to give him something to eat.
So my brother used to-- there was pieces of wood.
He used to tie it down around my waist under my shawl.
I had a shawl, I like a piece of rag to keep me warm.
And I used to go out from the gate.
Sometimes the-- what standing at the gate, he use-- he realized that I have some wood, something hidden.
He didn't pay any attention.
Some-- one of them [INAUDIBLE], and he opened that piece of rag, saw the pieces of wood tied to my naked body.
He used to tear it off together with my skin.
This was the life in Bershad.
One incident, when the Russians started already to attack, come closer, until then we had the Romanian.
And the Capitain Generale used to hang Jewish people on the trees.
And they were hanging for days, dangling there on the trees.
And then when the Germans came already closer became life even more terrible, because the SS already.
And I remember one night, I had to go to the washroom, [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't had any washroom, so we had to go out outside.
And this was in the night in the snow.
And I was practically naked.
The minute I went out, I saw the German patrol coming close.
I was 11 years old by then.
So what does 11-year-old do in such case?
I knew if I run back in, they will shoot me and who knows how many people with me.
So I lie down naked in the snow until the German patrol passed.
And then when I came in, into-- in the room, whatever you want to call it, I was shaking.
I was practically dead from fear and from cold.
But nobody paid attention.
Because a dead person, a dead Jew, a dead child didn't mean anything.
Because we knew this is going to be end of us.
We are going to be-- eventually all of us will be die-- will die.
I worked maybe a year or so.
That's right.
And the thing is, this little boy only to fall asleep outside in the fresh air.
So she used to wrap him up in a big blanket.
It wasn't any carriages like now we take a baby in a carriage.
She used to wrap it up in a big blanket.
I think the baby with the blankets were heavier and bigger than me.
But I had no choice.
I used to carry it in my hand until the child fell asleep.
Even carrying the child on the street, it was a-- in danger.
A Ukrainian militia, because they were collaborator Ukrainian, will-- with the whip will kill me.
A gendarme will shoot me no matter what.
But I had no choice.
It was a matter of survival.
Or you-- you go, you die.
You don't go, you die from hunger.
So this was-- most of the people, that's what they did.
It was either way, you die.
So that was the life in Bershad.
Nobody-- I was lucky.
I was lucky that I wasn't caught.
That was pure luck.
But-- and I'd be caught because this the 14-years-old boy, what I happen to know.
How many people that I don't remember were caught?
Because everybody knew that what's happening when we are going out of the ghetto.
But it was a question of survival.
No matter what, you will die.
So this was the life in-- in-- in Bershad.
I mean, there is all over.
I'm talking just Bershad.
It's one place that I have been.
Transnistria-- it was 300,000 people died in Transnistria.
They thing is that why is nobody know about Transnistria?
Because it was never on the map.
It was just a piece of land, Ukraine, and they gave it a name, that never existed.
And after the Russian came in 1944, it ceased to exist.
So that's why when people ask me, Freda, where have you been during the war, and I say Transnistria, they ask me trans-what, or trans-where?
I never heard about it.
This is very painful when you know that over 300,000 Jews, Romanian people, Jewish from Ukraine, died in this piece of land.
And nobody knew and heard about it.
This is very painful.
An author wrote a book.
It was called-- about Transnistria.
It was called
And that's exactly what it is.
Because all of Transnistria is like a big cemetery with mass graves-- no stone, no sign, no-- nothing.
Nobody didn't know what happens, where it happened.
So this is because when the Russian eventually liberated us, we all became Soviet citizen.
There's no mentioning nowhere that those Jewish survivor Romanian Jews.
But for the Romanian, we were the Communist.
For the Russians, we were who knows what-- never mentioned that we are Jewish survivors.
That's what happened.
This is Bershad.
TIll-- This is the place where I was.
Now in 1940, the end of 19--
Yeah.
